opere.descrizione: Painted in Saint-Remy as a gift for his newborn nephew, this branch against a vivid blue sky borrows its bold outlines from Japanese woodcuts. Almond trees bloom early in spring, making them a symbol of new life - the nephew, Vincent Willem, later founded this very museum.

A tangle of roots and trunks on a slope in Auvers-sur-Oise, rendered in restless, fragmented brushstrokes. Long believed to be Van Gogh's final painting, made the morning of the day he shot himself. Its exact location was identified in 2020 from an old postcard.

One of more than thirty self-portraits Van Gogh painted, mostly during his Paris years when he could not always afford models. The cool blue background and direct gaze are typical of this period, just before his palette exploded with color in Arles.

Painted as an anatomy study during his brief, frustrating time at the Antwerp academy, this small canvas reads as a dry joke at the expense of his teachers - a skeleton smoking, defiantly alive. The museum itself calls it a juvenile joke.

Van Gogh's room in the Yellow House in Arles, painted with deliberately flat perspective and clashing colors inspired by Japanese prints. He considered it one of his best compositions and painted three versions; this is the first.

Part of a series painted to decorate Gauguin's room in the Yellow House, this version shows fifteen sunflowers against a yellow background. Van Gogh signed his name on the vase itself, a rare gesture of pride in his own work.

Painted in Auvers weeks before his death, this turbulent sky over a wheatfield is often called his last work - a persistent myth, since several paintings followed it. Van Gogh wanted it to express both sorrow and what he called the healthy, fortifying nature of the countryside.

One of several self-portraits Warhol made throughout his career, this acid green and pink silkscreen turns the artist's own face into the same kind of repeatable, depersonalized image he used for celebrities and consumer goods. Part of the Edlis/Neeson gift, the largest in the museum's history.

Warhol returns to the most reproduced image in art history and reproduces it again, in his own silkscreen language. By multiplying the world's most famous smile, he flattens the line between masterpiece and souvenir.

Possibly the most parodied painting in American art, this portrait of a farmer and his daughter (often mistaken for his wife) was modeled on a real house in Eldon, Iowa, in the Carpenter Gothic style. Wood intended it as a sincere, if ambiguous, image of rural values.

Painted in the weeks after Pearl Harbor, this late-night diner scene became one of the defining images of American loneliness and quiet unease. Hopper said he was simply painting the loneliness of a large city.

opere.titolo: A Sunday on La Grande Jatte
opere.descrizione: Seurat spent two years applying tiny dots of pure color, a technique he called divisionism, to build this enormous scene of Parisians at leisure on an island in the Seine. Up close it dissolves into thousands of individual marks; from a distance, it reassembles into light.

Part of Koons' Banality series, this porcelain sculpture borrows the kitsch finish of 18th-century figurines for a crude, cartoonish joke. Like much of Koons' work, it sits deliberately between bad taste and fine art, refusing to pick a side.

One of thirteen canvases from the same publicity photo of Elizabeth Taylor, each set against a different jewel-toned background. Made the year of Taylor's near-fatal illness during the filming of Cleopatra, when she was as much a subject of public anxiety as admiration.

A study for a larger mural commissioned by the Russian collector Sergei Shchukin, reduced to just three colors. Matisse called the simplified palette and flattened figures essential to the painting's sense of release and rhythm.

Part of the series Monet painted in his garden at Giverny during his last decades, increasingly abstract as his eyesight failed. This triptych, nearly 42 feet wide when fully assembled, has no horizon line - only water, light, and lilies.

Two figures kiss with their heads wrapped in cloth, a recurring motif Magritte returned to several times. He never explained the veiled faces, leaving the painting suspended between intimacy and concealment.

Painted from memory and imagination at the asylum in Saint-Remy, looking out his window before sunrise. Van Gogh considered it a failure compared to his daytime work, yet it's now one of the most recognized paintings in the world.

opere.titolo: Unique Forms of Continuity in Space
opere.descrizione: A bronze figure striding forward, its surface dissolved into flame-like forms to express motion itself rather than a body in motion. Boccioni, a leading voice of Italian Futurism, died just two years later, at 33, after a fall from a horse.

Thirty-two canvases, one for every variety of soup Campbell's sold at the time, each hand-painted to look machine-printed. Warhol's first major Pop Art statement turned a supermarket shelf into a gallery wall.

Matisse painted over the original gray-green walls of his actual studio in flat red, leaving only outlines for the furniture and his own paintings shown in miniature on the walls. The room reads as memory more than a real place.

The inverted pyramid hangs above the underground mall beneath the Louvre like a question mark aimed at the original above. Pei designed both: the entrance pyramid in the courtyard and this smaller counterpoint below it, pointing down toward a miniature stone pyramid on the floor Ã¢ÂÂ a detail most visitors miss entirely.

She has no head, no arms, and still commands the entire staircase. Carved to stand on the prow of a stone ship, the drapery pressed flat against her body by wind that no longer blows Ã¢ÂÂ the sculptor froze a moment of arrival in marble so convincingly that the stillness reads as motion.

The painting is smaller than you expect and further away than you'd like, behind bulletproof glass and a rope barrier, surrounded by people photographing it with their backs turned. The famous smile survives all of this Ã¢ÂÂ a side effect of sfumato, Leonardo's technique of blurring outlines so the expression shifts depending on where your eye lands.

David painted this within months of Marat's assassination, while the Revolution was still in progress. The composition borrows from PietÃÂ  paintings Ã¢ÂÂ a deliberate political act, casting a journalist and political agitator as a secular martyr. The quill still in his hand, the letter from his killer still in the other.

Canova carved this from a single block of marble, which makes the interlocked arms and the void between the two faces Ã¢ÂÂ the moment just before the kiss Ã¢ÂÂ structurally improbable and visually breathtaking. The surface was finished to imitate skin, polished in some places, left rough in others.

Discovered on the island of Milos in 1820, the statue arrived in Paris within the year Ã¢ÂÂ a diplomatic gift that became one of the museum's defining works. The missing arms have been debated for two centuries; the leading theory places one reaching forward, one holding a shield. The marble background installed in the 1980s matches the reddish walls of the original room and is almost never noticed.
An Amarillo institution since 1960, famous for its free 72oz steak challenge. The oversized neon cowboy has been luring drivers off the interstate for decades.
Ten Cadillacs buried nose-first in a wheat field outside Amarillo. Visitors are encouraged to bring spray paint Ã¢ÂÂ the surface changes completely every few weeks.
Open since 1939, one of the last fully intact motor courts on the original route. The neon sign still lights up every night, visible for miles across the high desert.
Seligman is where the fight to preserve Route 66 started. Angel Delgadillo organized the Historic Route 66 Association in 1987 after the bypass killed the town overnight.
The sign still stands but the rooms have been empty for years. Truxton lost its traffic when Interstate 40 opened in 1978.
The original shield painted on the asphalt, cracked and bleached by decades of desert sun. One of the longest continuous sections of the original 1926 alignment still driveable.
Somewhere in the Mojave, a roadside stop offers cigarettes, reading glasses, and a hand-painted approximation of Starbucks. The real one is probably two hundred miles away. This one will do.
